questions.promptTex: P(\text{rolling a 5 on a fair die}) = ?
questions.explanation: $1$ favorable out of $6$ equally likely → $1/6$.
steps: Sample space: $\{1,2,3,4,5,6\}$ — 6 equally likely outcomes.
Favorable: rolling a 5 — 1 outcome.

questions.promptTex: P(\text{rolling an even number on a fair die}) = ?
questions.explanation: Evens are 2, 4, 6 (3 outcomes). $3/6 = 1/2$.
steps: Favorable outcomes (even): $\{2, 4, 6\}$ — 3 of them.
Complement: $P(A^c) = 1 - P(A) = 1 - 0.3 = 0.7$.

questions.promptTex: \text{Probability values must be in the range:}
hints: Probability can't be negative or above 1.
questions.explanation: Probabilities are always in $[0, 1]$. $0$ = impossible; $1$ = certain.
steps: $P = 0$: event cannot happen.
$P = 1$: event must happen.
All other probabilities are between these.

questions.promptTex: \text{What is } P(\text{getting heads on at least one of 2 fair coin flips})?
hints: Sample space: HH, HT, TH, TT (4 outcomes).
Favorable (at least 1 H): HH, HT, TH (3 of 4).
questions.explanation: 3 of 4 outcomes contain at least one H: $3/4$.
steps: Sample space for 2 flips: $\{HH, HT, TH, TT\}$ — 4 equally likely.
Favorable (at least one H): $HH, HT, TH$ — 3 of 4.
(Or via complement: $P(\text{no heads}) = P(TT) = 1/4$, so $P(\geq 1\, H) = 1 - 1/4 = 3/4$.)

questions.promptTex: \text{A bag has 3 red, 4 blue, 5 green marbles. Pick one at random.} \\ P(\text{red}) = ? \text{ (decimal, round to 3 places)}
steps: Total marbles: $3 + 4 + 5 = 12$.
Favorable: 3 red.

questions.explanation: $P(\text{not red}) = 1 - P(\text{red}) = 1 - 0.25 = 0.75$.
steps: Complement: $P(\text{not red}) = 1 - P(\text{red}) = 1 - 0.25 = 0.75$.
Or directly: $9$ marbles are not red, $9/12 = 0.75$.

questions.promptTex: P(\text{at least one 6 in 3 rolls of a fair die}) \\ \text{(round to 3 decimals)}.
questions.explanation: $P(\text{no 6 in 3}) = (5/6)^3 \approx 0.579$. So $P(\geq 1) \approx 0.421$.
steps: Complement strategy: easier to count 'no 6 in 3 rolls' than 'at least one 6'.
Each roll: $P(\text{not 6}) = 5/6$.
Three rolls (independent): $(5/6)^3 = 125/216 \approx 0.579$.

questions.promptTex: \text{Theoretical probability of heads on a fair coin is 0.5. After 100 flips,} \\ \text{which is most likely the empirical probability?}
hints: Empirical probability fluctuates around the theoretical, but tends to be close after many trials.
100 is moderately many — close, but not exactly.
questions.explanation: After 100 trials, empirical probability is typically close to but not exactly 0.5. With 1000+ trials it gets even tighter.
steps: Law of large numbers: empirical probability approaches theoretical as $n$ grows.
100 flips: usually within a few percentage points of 0.5 (typical range 0.42–0.58).
Very rarely exactly 0.5 (would require exactly 50 H and 50 T).
Almost never far from 0.5 (e.g., 10 heads out of 100 is statistically very unusual).

questions.promptTex: \text{Two fair coins are flipped.} \\ P(\text{exactly one head}) = ? \text{ (decimal)}
hints: Sample space: HH, HT, TH, TT (4 outcomes).
Exactly one H: HT or TH (2 of 4) → 1/2.
questions.explanation: Outcomes with exactly one H: HT, TH (2 of 4). $2/4 = 0.5$.
steps: Sample space (2 flips, ordered): $\{HH, HT, TH, TT\}$.
Exactly one head: $\{HT, TH\}$ — 2 outcomes.

questions.promptTex: \text{If } P(A) + P(B) > 1, \text{ then events } A \text{ and } B:
hints: If sum > 1, they MUST share outcomes.
questions.explanation: If $P(A) + P(B) > 1$, the events must share outcomes. Otherwise total would exceed 1.
steps: If $A$ and $B$ are mutually exclusive (no overlap), $P(A) + P(B) \leq 1$.
If $P(A) + P(B) > 1$, they cannot be mutually exclusive — they must share outcomes.
**They must overlap.**

questions.promptTex: \text{A spinner has 8 equal sectors, numbered 1–8.} \\ P(\text{spinning a multiple of 3}) = ? \text{ (decimal)}
steps: Total outcomes: 8.
Multiples of 3 in {1, 2, …, 8}: $3, 6$ — 2 outcomes.

questions.promptTex: \text{Two fair dice are rolled. What is the SAMPLE SPACE (the set of all possible outcomes)?}
hints: The sample space is the set of all *equally likely* outcomes.
Two dice are distinguishable (think of them as red and blue). (3, 5) ≠ (5, 3) — different outcomes.
questions.explanation: Two distinguishable dice give $6 \times 6 = 36$ equally likely ordered pairs. The set of sums $\{2, 3, \ldots, 12\}$ is NOT a valid sample space because those outcomes aren't equally likely (e.g., sum 7 has 6 ways, sum 12 has just 1). The face values describe one die, not two. Unordered pairs would under-count because (3, 5) and (5, 3) are distinct dice events.
steps: **Recognize the requirement.** A *sample space* is a set of *equally likely* outcomes that exhaustively covers everything that can happen. Equal likelihood is the key requirement — probabilities then come from counting.

steps.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 280 220" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><text x="140" y="18" font-size="11" fill="#475569" font-style="italic" text-anchor="middle">die 2 →</text><text x="12" y="110" font-size="11" fill="#475569" font-style="italic" text-anchor="middle" transform="rotate(-90 12 110)">die 1 →</text><text x="63" y="36" font-size="11" font-weight="700" fill="#0f172a" text-anchor="middle">1</text><text x="96" y="36" font-size="11" font-weight="700" fill="#0f172a" text-anchor="middle">2</text><text x="129" y="36" font-size="11" font-weight="700" fill="#0f172a" text-anchor="middle">3</text><text x="162" y="36" font-size="11" font-weight="700" fill="#0f172a" text-anchor="middle">4</text><text x="195" y="36" font-size="11" font-weight="700" fill="#0f172a" text-anchor="middle">5</text><text x="228" y="36" font-size="11" font-weight="700" fill="#0f172a" text-anchor="middle">6</text><text x="35" y="60" font-size="11" font-weight="700" fill="#0f172a" text-anchor="middle">1</text><text x="35" y="83" font-size="11" font-weight="700" fill="#0f172a" text-anchor="middle">2</text><text x="35" y="106" font-size="11" font-weight="700" fill="#0f172a" text-anchor="middle">3</text><text x="35" y="129" font-size="11" font-weight="700" fill="#0f172a" text-anchor="middle">4</text><text x="35" y="152" font-size="11" font-weight="700" fill="#0f172a" text-anchor="middle">5</text><text x="35" y="175" font-size="11" font-weight="700" fill="#0f172a" text-anchor="middle">6</text><rect x="48" y="43" width="196" height="138" fill="#fef3c7" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="0.7"/><line x1="48" y1="66" x2="244" y2="66" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="0.5"/><line x1="48" y1="89" x2="244" y2="89" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="0.5"/><line x1="48" y1="112" x2="244" y2="112" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="0.5"/><line x1="48" y1="135" x2="244" y2="135" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="0.5"/><line x1="48" y1="158" x2="244" y2="158" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="0.5"/><line x1="81" y1="43" x2="81" y2="181" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="0.5"/><line x1="114" y1="43" x2="114" y2="181" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="0.5"/><line x1="147" y1="43" x2="147" y2="181" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="0.5"/><line x1="180" y1="43" x2="180" y2="181" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="0.5"/><line x1="213" y1="43" x2="213" y2="181" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="0.5"/><text x="63" y="59" font-size="9" fill="#0f172a" text-anchor="middle">(1,1)</text><text x="129" y="105" font-size="9" fill="#0f172a" text-anchor="middle">(3,3)</text><text x="228" y="174" font-size="9" fill="#0f172a" text-anchor="middle">(6,6)</text><text x="30" y="203" font-size="11" fill="#475569" font-style="italic">36 cells, each equally likely (probability 1/36)</text></svg>

steps: **Why not the 11 sums?** Sums {2, ..., 12} are NOT equally likely. Sum 7 happens 6 different ways: (1,6), (2,5), (3,4), (4,3), (5,2), (6,1). Sum 12 happens just one way: (6,6). Treating sums as equally likely would be wrong.
**Conclusion.** The 36 ordered pairs $\{(i, j) : 1 \leq i, j \leq 6\}$ are equally likely (each has probability $1/36$) — that's the genuine sample space.

**Pattern lesson.** When the problem mentions distinguishable objects (two dice, two coins, two cards), keep them distinguishable. Pretending order doesn't matter loses information and breaks the equal-likelihood property.

questions.promptTex: \text{Two fair dice are rolled. } P(\text{sum} = 7) = ? \\ \text{(Give a decimal, rounded to 3 places.)}
hints: Pairs summing to 7: (1,6), (2,5), (3,4), (4,3), (5,2), (6,1) — exactly 6 of them.
questions.explanation: $6$ ordered pairs sum to $7$: $(1,6), (2,5), (3,4), (4,3), (5,2), (6,1)$. Out of $36$ equally likely pairs: $P = 6/36 = 1/6 \approx 0.167$. This makes 7 the most likely sum — exactly because 7 sits in the middle, where the most pairs cancel out.
steps: **Use the right sample space.** $6 \times 6 = 36$ ordered pairs, all equally likely.

stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="0.7"/><rect x="48" y="158" width="33" height="23" fill="#fca5a5" stroke="#b91c1c" stroke-width="1.2"/><rect x="81" y="135" width="33" height="23" fill="#fca5a5" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="0.5"/><text x="63" y="174" font-size="10" font-weight="700" fill="#7f1d1d" text-anchor="middle">7</text><text x="96" y="151" font-size="10" font-weight="700" fill="#7f1d1d" text-anchor="middle">7</text><text x="129" y="128" font-size="10" font-weight="700" fill="#7f1d1d" text-anchor="middle">7</text><text x="162" y="105" font-size="10" font-weight="700" fill="#7f1d1d" text-anchor="middle">7</text><text x="195" y="82" font-size="10" font-weight="700" fill="#7f1d1d" text-anchor="middle">7</text><text x="228" y="59" font-size="10" font-weight="700" fill="#7f1d1d" text-anchor="middle">7</text><text x="30" y="203" font-size="11" fill="#b91c1c" font-style="italic">6 of 36 cells sum to 7 (anti-diagonal)</text></svg>

steps: **Count favorable outcomes.** Pairs $(i, j)$ with $i + j = 7$: $(1,6), (2,5), (3,4), (4,3), (5,2), (6,1)$ — exactly 6.

**Why is 7 the peak?** The number of pairs summing to $k$ peaks at $k = 7$ (center of the range 2–12) because that's where the most $(i, j)$ combinations fit. The probability distribution of the sum is a *triangular* shape with peak at 7.

questions.promptTex: \text{A standard deck has 52 cards: 13 ranks } (A, 2, \ldots, 10, J, Q, K) \text{ in 4 suits.} \\ \text{Drawing one card at random, } P(\text{face card, i.e. } J, Q, \text{ or } K) = ? \\ \text{Round to 3 decimals.}
hints: Face cards: J, Q, K. That's 3 face ranks per suit, 4 suits → 12 face cards.
questions.explanation: Face cards are J, Q, K — 3 ranks. Each rank has 4 suits, so $3 \times 4 = 12$ face cards in the deck of $52$. $P = 12/52 = 3/13 \approx 0.231$.
steps: **Count favorable.** Face cards = J, Q, K. That's $3$ ranks $\times\ 4$ suits = $12$ face cards.
**Count total.** Sample space: all $52$ cards in the deck, equally likely.
**Divide.** $P = \dfrac{12}{52} = \dfrac{3}{13} \approx 0.231$.

**Useful mental anchors for card probabilities.** $P(\text{ace}) = 4/52 = 1/13 \approx 0.077$. $P(\text{red}) = 26/52 = 1/2$. $P(\text{specific suit}) = 13/52 = 1/4$. $P(\text{face card}) = 12/52 = 3/13$. $P(\text{specific card, e.g., ace of spades}) = 1/52$.

questions.prompt: Events A and B are BOTH mutually exclusive AND independent. What must be true?
choices: Always — these are essentially the same property
At least one of P(A) or P(B) must be 0
hints: Combining: $P(A) P(B) = 0$ → one of them is 0.
questions.explanation: Mutually exclusive ⇒ $P(A \cap B) = 0$. Independent ⇒ $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$. Combining: $P(A) \cdot P(B) = 0$, so $P(A) = 0$ or $P(B) = 0$. In other words: two events with positive probability cannot be both mutually exclusive AND independent. They're DIFFERENT properties.
steps:
**Distinguish the two terms.**

• *Mutually exclusive* = $A$ and $B$ cannot both happen at once. $P(A \cap B) = 0$.

• *Independent* = knowing $A$ happens doesn't change the probability of $B$. $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$.
**Combine.** If both hold simultaneously: $0 = P(A) \cdot P(B)$. So at least one is zero.

**Concrete example.** $A$ = 'die shows 1', $B$ = 'die shows 6'. Mutually exclusive (can't both happen on one roll). Independent? $P(A \cap B) = 0$ but $P(A) \cdot P(B) = 1/36 \neq 0$. So NOT independent. The terms describe different things.

**Common confusion.** Many students treat 'mutually exclusive' and 'independent' as synonyms or as opposites. They're neither — they're orthogonal concepts. Memorize the test: $P(A \cap B) = 0$ vs $P(A \cap B) = P(A) P(B)$.

questions.promptTex: \text{Events: } P(A) = 0.6, P(B) = 0.5, P(A \cap B) = 0.2. \\ \text{Find } P(A \cup B) \text{ (the probability that A or B (or both) occurs).}
hints: Inclusion-exclusion: $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$.
questions.explanation: Inclusion-exclusion formula: $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$. Adding $P(A)$ and $P(B)$ double-counts the intersection, so we subtract it once. With the given values: $0.6 + 0.5 - 0.2 = 0.9$.
steps: **The inclusion-exclusion formula.** $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$.

steps.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 280 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="105" cy="100" r="55" fill="rgba(37,99,235,0.25)" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="1.6"/><circle cx="175" cy="100" r="55" fill="rgba(220,38,38,0.25)" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="1.6"/><text x="75" y="105" font-size="13" font-weight="600" fill="#1e3a8a">A</text><text x="195" y="105" font-size="13" font-weight="600" fill="#991b1b">B</text><text x="140" y="105" font-size="11" fill="#0f172a" font-weight="600" text-anchor="middle">A ∩ B</text><text x="50" y="175" font-size="12" fill="#0f172a" font-style="italic">P(A ∪ B) = P(A) + P(B) − P(A ∩ B)</text></svg>

steps: **Why subtract the intersection?** When you add $P(A)$ and $P(B)$, the overlap region $A \cap B$ is counted twice (once in $A$, once in $B$). Subtract it once to get the correct $P(A \cup B)$.
**Sanity bounds.** $P(A \cup B)$ is always $\geq \max(P(A), P(B))$ (here $\geq 0.6$ ✓) and $\leq P(A) + P(B)$ (here $\leq 1.1$, and bounded by 1 anyway). $0.9$ comfortably fits.

**Special case: mutually exclusive.** If $A$ and $B$ are mutually exclusive, $P(A \cap B) = 0$ and inclusion-exclusion reduces to $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B)$. That's the elementary 'addition rule' you learn first.

questions.promptTex: \text{A point is chosen uniformly at random inside a } 10 \times 10 \text{ square.} \\ \text{What is the probability that the point lies inside the inscribed circle of radius 5? Round to 3 decimals.}
hints: For uniform random points in a region, $P(\text{in subregion}) = \dfrac{\text{area of subregion}}{\text{total area}}$.
questions.explanation: *Geometric probability*: probabilities are ratios of areas. Circle area $= \pi \cdot 5^2 = 25\pi$. Square area $= 100$. $P = 25\pi / 100 = \pi/4 \approx 0.785$.
steps: **Recognize the type — geometric probability.** When all points are equally likely (uniform distribution over a region), probabilities are area (or length, or volume) ratios.

steps.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 280 220" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><rect x="50" y="30" width="160" height="160" fill="#fef3c7" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="1.5"/><circle cx="130" cy="110" r="80" fill="rgba(37,99,235,0.25)" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="42" y="205" font-size="12" font-weight="600" fill="#0f172a">10</text><text x="205" y="205" font-size="12" font-weight="600" fill="#0f172a">10</text><text x="130" y="114" font-size="12" font-weight="600" fill="#1e3a8a" text-anchor="middle">circle</text><text x="50" y="210" font-size="11" fill="#475569" font-style="italic">P = (area of circle) / (area of square)</text></svg>

steps: **Compute the two areas.** Square area $= 10^2 = 100$. Circle area $= \pi \cdot 5^2 = 25\pi$.
**Take the ratio.** $P = \dfrac{25\pi}{100} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \approx 0.7854$.

**Pattern lesson.** $\pi/4$ appears whenever you inscribe a circle in a square — it's a 'universal constant' you'll see again in Buffon's needle, the Monte Carlo estimation of $\pi$ (throw darts at a square, count the fraction landing in the circle, multiply by 4), and the geometric meaning of the Gaussian integral.

questions.promptTex: \text{A biased coin lands heads with probability } 0.7 \text{ (and tails with } 0.3). \\ \text{The coin is flipped twice (independent flips).} \\ P(\text{both heads}) = ? \text{ Give a decimal.}
hints: Independent events: multiply probabilities for AND.
questions.explanation: Independent flips: $P(\text{HH}) = P(H) \cdot P(H) = 0.7 \cdot 0.7 = 0.49$. The probability tree below shows all four outcomes; the 'H,H' branch has total probability $0.49$.
steps: **Recognize the type — independent compound events.** For independent events, multiply probabilities along each branch of a tree.

steps.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 280 220" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="40" cy="110" r="6" fill="#1e293b"/><line x1="46" y1="105" x2="110" y2="55" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="46" y1="115" x2="110" y2="165" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="1.5"/><circle cx="120" cy="50" r="6" fill="#2563eb"/><circle cx="120" cy="170" r="6" fill="#dc2626"/><text x="75" y="70" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb" font-weight="700">0.7 H</text><text x="75" y="155" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626" font-weight="700">0.3 T</text><line x1="126" y1="45" x2="205" y2="30" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="126" y1="55" x2="205" y2="75" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="126" y1="165" x2="205" y2="145" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="126" y1="175" x2="205" y2="195" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="160" y="30" font-size="10" fill="#2563eb" font-weight="600">0.7 H</text><text x="160" y="77" font-size="10" fill="#dc2626" font-weight="600">0.3 T</text><text x="160" y="143" font-size="10" fill="#2563eb" font-weight="600">0.7 H</text><text x="160" y="205" font-size="10" fill="#dc2626" font-weight="600">0.3 T</text><text x="212" y="32" font-size="11" font-weight="700" fill="#16a34a">HH: 0.49</text><text x="212" y="79" font-size="11" fill="#0f172a">HT: 0.21</text><text x="212" y="147" font-size="11" fill="#0f172a">TH: 0.21</text><text x="212" y="199" font-size="11" fill="#0f172a">TT: 0.09</text></svg>

steps: **Multiply along the HH branch.** $P(\text{HH}) = 0.7 \cdot 0.7 = 0.49$.
**Sanity check — all four probabilities sum to 1.** $0.49 + 0.21 + 0.21 + 0.09 = 1.00$ ✓

**Pattern lesson.** For *independent* events $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n$: $P(A_1 \cap A_2 \cap \cdots \cap A_n) = P(A_1) \cdot P(A_2) \cdots P(A_n)$. Multiplication of probabilities is the operational signature of independence.

questions.promptTex: \text{The ODDS in favor of an event are quoted as } 3 \text{ to } 2 \text{ (written } 3:2). \\ \text{What is the probability of the event? Give a decimal.}
hints: Odds $a : b$ in favor → probability $= a/(a+b)$.
questions.explanation: Odds $a : b$ in favor mean: for every $a$ expected occurrences, there are $b$ non-occurrences. Total 'slots' is $a + b$. So $P = a/(a+b) = 3/5 = 0.6$. Conversely, given $P$, odds in favor are $P : (1-P)$.
steps: **Translate odds to probability.** Odds $a : b$ in favor of an event → think 'in $a + b$ trials, expect $a$ wins and $b$ losses'. Probability of a single win: $\dfrac{a}{a + b}$.
**Reverse conversion.** Given a probability $p$, the odds in favor are $p : (1-p)$. For $p = 0.6$: odds = $0.6 : 0.4 = 3 : 2$ ✓.
**Watch for 'against' vs 'in favor'.** Gambling commonly quotes odds AGAINST ('3:1 against' means $P = 1/(3+1) = 1/4$, not $3/4$). Always confirm the direction before converting.
